evals.prompt: I'm building a 2D platformer in Godot 4.6 with GDScript. My player has health, a hurtbox, and several abilities (dash, double jump, fireball). Right now it's all in one 600-line player.gd and the scene is getting unwieldy. How should I restructure this? Show me the scene tree and the key GDScript patterns.
evals.expected_output: Split the player into focused child-node components: a HealthComponent (Node) emitting typed signals like health_changed(new_health: int, max_health: int) and died, a HurtboxComponent (Area2D), and each ability as a child Node extending a base Ability class with a _execute() hook. The StateMachine node owns State child nodes (IdleState, RunState, JumpState) wired via @export — not a match block in _process. The player.gd connects to component signals in _ready using Callable syntax. Deep get_node path strings like get_node('../../HUD/HealthBar') are replaced with @export references or signals.
expectations: Recommends HealthComponent as a separate Node child (not inline logic in player.gd) emitting typed signals health_changed(new_health: int, max_health: int) and died
Recommends a StateMachine node owning State child nodes per state (IdleState, RunState, JumpState) with states wired via @export, explicitly warning against a match block in _process
Recommends abilities as child Nodes extending a base Ability class with a _execute() hook and cooldown ticking in _process, not as methods on player.gd
Signals are connected in _ready using Callable syntax ($HealthComponent.health_changed.connect(...)), not in _process
Avoids recommending get_node path strings like get_node('../../HUD/HealthBar'); uses @export references or signals for cross-node communication instead

evals.prompt: My Godot 4.6 game has a bullet hell phase where I'm spawning and queue_free-ing hundreds of bullets per second. Performance is dropping. I also need to share bullet stats (damage, speed, sprite) across all bullets of the same type without duplicating data. Walk me through the right Godot patterns for both problems.
evals.expected_output: Use an Object Pool: pre-instantiate bullets in _ready, set visible = false and process_mode = Node.PROCESS_MODE_DISABLED to deactivate them, and re-enable rather than instantiate/free. For shared stats, define a BulletData Resource subclass with @export fields (damage, speed, sprite_frames) and assign the same .tres file to all bullets of that type — this is the Flyweight pattern via Godot's resource system. Warn that modifying a shared Resource at runtime mutates it for all holders; use resource.duplicate() for per-instance overrides.
expectations: Describes the Object Pool as pre-instantiated nodes toggled with visible = false and process_mode = Node.PROCESS_MODE_DISABLED, not queue_free/instantiate per bullet
Describes the Flyweight as a custom Resource subclass (e.g. BulletData extends Resource) with @export fields, where all bullets of the same type share one .tres file
Warns that modifying a shared Resource at runtime changes it for every node holding that resource, and that resource.duplicate(true) is required for per-instance data
Specifies that the pool should reset bullet state (position, velocity, damage) when retrieving a bullet from the pool
Avoids recommending a Dictionary or plain GDScript class for the shared stats data — uses a typed Resource subclass

evals.prompt: I'm adding a coroutine in Godot 4.6 GDScript that plays a hurt animation, waits for it to finish, then plays a death animation, waits 0.5 seconds, and finally calls queue_free(). I also need a separate function that waits for the player to press a button within 5 seconds or times out. What are the correct patterns and what can go wrong?
evals.expected_output: After every await, check is_instance_valid(self) before accessing nodes — the node can be freed externally (scene change, level reset) while the coroutine is suspended. The death sequence uses await $AnimationPlayer.animation_finished then await get_tree().create_timer(0.5).timeout before queue_free(). The timeout pattern cannot use Promise.race; instead use a responded flag set by both a CONNECT_ONE_SHOT signal connection and a timer, then poll with while not responded: await get_tree().process_frame, checking is_instance_valid(self) inside the loop.
expectations: Requires an is_instance_valid(self) guard after every await, explaining that the node can be freed externally during suspension
Uses await get_tree().create_timer(0.5).timeout (not a Timer node or OS.delay_msec) for the 0.5-second wait before queue_free()

Describes the timeout pattern using a responded boolean flag set by CONNECT_ONE_SHOT connections on both the input signal and the timer, polled with while not responded: await get_tree().process_frame
Notes that Godot has no Promise.race equivalent and the flag-plus-poll loop is the idiomatic substitute
Includes is_instance_valid(self) inside the polling loop of the timeout function, not just outside it
